message.content:
The air in Mr. Thorne’s shop was thick with the scent of old paper, brass polish, and dust motes dancing in the slanted afternoon light. The room was a cacophony of ticks, tocks, chimes, and whirs, a symphony of mechanical hearts beating in slightly different rhythms.

Leo, seven years old and vibrating with the restless energy of a Tuesday afternoon, pressed his nose against the glass case of a grandfather clock that hadn’t worked since 1924.

"Mr. Thorne?" Leo asked, his voice barely rising above the ticking. "Why does it always go *tick-tock, tick-tock*? Why doesn’t it just go *tick*?"

Mr. Thorne looked up from his workbench, where he was bent over a pocket watch the size of a saucer. He adjusted his spectacles, the lenses magnifying his eyes to comical proportions. "Because, Leo, time needs a heartbeat. It needs a rhythm to march to. Without the *tock*, the *tick* has nowhere to go."

Leo frowned, tilting his head. "But time isn’t a person, Mr. Thorne. It doesn’t have legs to march."

"No, it doesn't," Thorne agreed, setting down his tweezers. He wiped his hands on a rag and beckoned Leo closer. "Come here. Tell me, what is time to you?"

Leo thought hard, counting the seconds on his fingers. "It’s when I have to go to bed. It’s when my ice cream melts. It’s when the playground gets empty."

Thorne smiled, a network of wrinkles forming around his mouth. "And what is time to this clock?" He pointed to the intricate gears exposed on his workbench.

"It’s... movement?" Leo guessed.
